comments:
Summary
This paper studies a broad class of sentence-pair tasks (answer sentence selection, next-utterance ranking, STS, paraphrase ID) from a unified perspective, provides a common open-source framework (dataset-sts / KeraSTS) for running a variety of neural models, introduces (or reuses) larger/harder datasets (yodaqa variants) and reports cross-task comparisons of CNN, RNN, hybrid and attention models. The authors additionally emphasize evaluation methodology for randomized neural models (multiple runs, 95% CIs) and report transfer-learning experiments (pretraining on Ubuntu dialogue improves answer-selection performance).

Strengths
- The unifying perspective on many sentence-pair tasks is timely and valuable: treating them as instances of an f2(s0,s1) scoring problem simplifies comparisons and suggests reuse of models.
- Providing an open-source framework and (larger) datasets should help reproducibility and future work in the community.
- The focus on evaluation methodology (multiple randomized runs, reporting confidence intervals) addresses an important and often-neglected issue in neural NLP benchmarking.
- The transfer-learning experiment (Ubuntu -> answer selection) is convincing evidence of cross-task reusability of learned representations.

Weaknesses / Concerns
- Experimental completeness: the authors themselves note that some reported numbers are not final and some evaluations (e.g. Ubuntu) are single-sample rather than the full repeated-run estimates. This undermines immediate reproducibility and confidence in comparisons.
- Comparisons to state-of-the-art are uneven. Several well-known datasets and model classes (e.g., SNLI/RTE, serial attention RNNs, skip-thoughts or other sentence-embedding approaches) are acknowledged but left out; this limits assessment of how far the presented baselines are from the true state-of-the-art.
- Dataset issues: the paper mentions overlap between wang and yodaqa which complicates mixing training and test splits; the potential for subtle leakage should be more thoroughly analyzed and quantified.
- Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping appear to be driven primarily on a single dataset (yodaqa/curatedv2). It is unclear whether this biases results in cross-task comparisons. The paper also reports heavy overfitting and early stopping after few epochs—more discussion about regularization, validation strategies and fairness across architectures is needed.
- Presentation: the provided text has some duplication, formatting glitches and repetitions which make reading harder (this may be an artifact of the submission text here, but it reduces clarity). More careful organization of tables and final numeric results is needed.

Recommendations for Improvement
- Finalize experiments and present all reported numbers with full repeated-run statistics (means and 95% CIs) for all datasets and models to allow meaningful comparisons.
- Include additional major datasets and model variants (SNLI/RTE, serial attention RNNs, skip-thoughts / pretrained sentence encoders) or explicitly motivate their exclusion.
- Quantify dataset overlaps and ensure train/test splits are clean; provide checks to prevent leakage across datasets derived from similar question pools.
- Provide a clearer description of hyperparameter tuning, validation procedures and whether any dataset-specific tuning occurred; consider per-task tuning to avoid bias.
- Release code, dataset splits, and exact random seeds used for the reported runs to maximize reproducibility.

Verdict
This paper presents useful and broadly applicable contributions: a unifying framing, an open framework, attention to statistical evaluation of randomized models, and promising transfer-learning results. The experimental work appears sound in conception but needs completion and clearer presentation before definitive claims can be fully validated. I recommend acceptance contingent on completing and cleaning up the experimental results and addressing the concerns above.
